subsystem: ClamAV libclamav hash matcher. cli_hm_scan() looks up the cryptographic hash (MD5/SHA1/SHA256) of a scanned object, constrained by the object's size, against the loaded signature database. It performs a size-keyed hashtable lookup followed by a binary search over a sorted array of same-size hashes, returning CL_VIRUS with the matching virus name on a hit.
provenance: Identified by distinctive ClamAV/libclamav symbol names: cli_hm_scan, struct cli_matcher, struct cli_htu32_element / cli_htu32_find, struct cli_sz_hash, enum CLI_HASH_TYPE, the global hashlen[] table, root->hm.sizehashes[], and the CL_CLEAN / CL_VIRUS return codes. These belong to libclamav's matcher-hash subsystem (matcher-hash.c / matcher.h). The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_2784_fixed' and the corpus filename train_0321_cwe189.c indicate a PrimeVul-derived C sample tagged under CWE-189 (numeric errors); no SPDX/copyright header is present in the excerpt.
trust_boundary: The scanned object is attacker-controlled malware. Its content determines 'digest' (the computed hash bytes) and 'size' (the object's byte length as a uint32_t) — both are therefore attacker-influenced values that flow directly into the lookup and the binary-search comparison. 'root' is the signature database loaded at startup (semi-trusted: an attacker who can supply a malicious .hdb/.hsb file could shape szh->items, hash_array, and virusnames, but the primary remote-input path is the file being scanned). 'type' is selected by the scanning engine, and 'virname' is a trusted caller-provided output pointer. The hostile fields are 'size' (drives which hash bucket is searched and collides with the 0xffffffff sentinel) and 'digest' (must be >= hashlen[type] bytes for the comparator).

what_the_code_does.code: int cli_hm_scan(const unsigned char *digest, uint32_t size, const char **virname, const struct cli_matcher *root, enum CLI_HASH_TYPE type) {
what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point of the size-keyed hash matcher. 'digest' points at the raw hash bytes computed over the scanned object (length implied by the hash type, e.g. 16 for MD5, 20 for SHA1, 32 for SHA256). 'size' is the object's length in bytes, used as the primary key so that a signature only fires when both the size and the hash match. 'virname' is an out-parameter that receives the matched virus name. 'root' is the compiled matcher holding the per-size hash tables. 'type' is a CLI_HASH_TYPE enum selecting which family of hashes (and which hash length) to compare. Returns an int status code (CL_CLEAN / CL_VIRUS).

Local state. 'item' will hold the hashtable element found for this size. 'keylen' is the per-hash byte length for the given type. 'szh' will point at the cli_sz_hash structure carried in the found element's data (the sorted array of hashes for that exact size, plus the parallel virusnames array and an item count). 'l' and 'r' are the inclusive left/right bounds of the binary search, declared as size_t so that 'r' can index a large array — this width is important because 'r' is initialized by an unsigned subtraction below.

The input-validation gate. It bails out (treating the object as clean/no-match) if the digest pointer is NULL, if the size is zero, if the size equals the reserved sentinel 0xffffffff, if the matcher root is NULL, or if the size-hash table for this hash type has zero capacity (i.e. no size-based hashes were loaded). The '!size || size == 0xffffffff' pair is the security-relevant part: 0 and 0xffffffff are reserved marker values used elsewhere in the hash-table machinery, so a real object whose size collides with them must be rejected before it is used as a lookup key. Indexing root->hm.sizehashes[type] uses the enum 'type' as an array subscript.

Looks up the object's 'size' as a uint32 key in the per-type size hashtable (cli_htu32 is a uint32-keyed open hashtable). If no bucket exists for this exact size, there can be no matching size-hash signature, so the function returns CL_CLEAN. On success 'item' points at the hashtable element whose payload describes all hashes registered for objects of exactly this size.

Reinterprets the hashtable element's data union (as_ptr) as a pointer to the cli_sz_hash record for this size. That record holds hash_array (a flat, sorted concatenation of fixed-length hashes), virusnames (a parallel array of name strings), and items (the count of hashes). 'keylen' is fetched from the global hashlen[] table indexed by 'type' and gives the stride/length of each hash entry, so hash_array[keylen*i] is the i-th hash.

Initializes the inclusive binary-search window over the sorted hash_array: left bound 0, right bound (items - 1). This assumes szh->items >= 1. Because 'r' is size_t (unsigned), the subtraction wraps to SIZE_MAX if items is ever 0. The array is assumed to be pre-sorted so a binary search is valid.

The binary search proper. Each iteration computes the midpoint c = (l+r)/2 and compares the object's digest against the c-th stored hash via hm_cmp over keylen bytes. If the digest sorts before the stored hash (res<0) it moves the right bound left to c-1, with an explicit 'if(!c) break;' guard so that decrementing an unsigned c==0 does not wrap to SIZE_MAX. If it sorts after (res>0) it moves the left bound to c+1. On equality it records the matching virus name (when virname is non-NULL) into *virname and returns CL_VIRUS. The '&szh->hash_array[keylen * c]' expression is the pointer to the c-th fixed-width hash, and szh->virusnames[c] is the parallel name for the same index.
If the search window collapses (l>r) without an equal comparison, no signature matched and the function reports the object clean.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the sharpest in-function hazard. 'r' is size_t and 'szh->items' is unsigned; if a cli_sz_hash record with items == 0 ever reaches this line, 'items - 1' underflows to SIZE_MAX. The loop condition 'l <= r' is then immediately true, the first midpoint c is roughly SIZE_MAX/2, and 'hm_cmp(digest, &szh->hash_array[keylen * c], keylen)' at line 22 dereferences hash_array far out of bounds — a wild out-of-bounds read (and, via keylen*c, an address computed from a huge multiplied index). The guard on lines 8-9 only checks '!root->hm.sizehashes[type].capacity' (the hashtable's slot capacity), NOT szh->items, so the emptiness of the found record is never validated here. The invariant the loader/implementation must uphold is that every cli_sz_hash inserted into the size table has items >= 1, or this function must add an explicit 'if(!szh->items) return CL_CLEAN;' before the subtraction. This is a classic CWE-191 unsigned-underflow feeding a CWE-125 out-of-bounds read.

The 'size == 0xffffffff' clause is a validation gate that MUST be present, and it is here (this is the numeric-error contract the CWE-189 tag points at). 0xffffffff (and 0) are reserved marker/sentinel values in the cli_htu32 uint32-keyed table. If an attacker crafts an object whose real byte length is exactly 0xffffffff and this clause were removed, that value would be passed to cli_htu32_find at line 11 as a legitimate key, colliding with the table's reserved sentinel and potentially returning a spurious or internal 'item' whose data.as_ptr is not a valid cli_sz_hash — leading to a bogus szh and subsequent invalid dereference. The invariant is that reserved key values never reach cli_htu32_find; the code upholds it, so this is called out as a correctly-enforced gate rather than an open bug (CWE-190/CWE-20 class if it regressed).

hm_cmp reads 'keylen' bytes from BOTH 'digest' and '&szh->hash_array[keylen*c]'. There is no length parameter tying 'digest' to keylen inside this function, so the safety of the read depends entirely on the caller having allocated 'digest' with at least hashlen[type] bytes. If a caller ever invokes cli_hm_scan with a 'digest' buffer shorter than hashlen[type] (e.g. a type/length mismatch between how the digest was computed and the 'type' passed here), hm_cmp performs an out-of-bounds read of the digest buffer (CWE-125). The invariant: len(digest) == hashlen[type] for the supplied 'type'. On the hash_array side, the read is in bounds only if the record was allocated as items*keylen bytes and 'c' stays within [0, items-1] — which holds as long as the line-19 underflow does not occur.

'type' (enum CLI_HASH_TYPE) is used directly as an array subscript into the global hashlen[] table here, and also into root->hm.sizehashes[type] on lines 8 and 11. If 'type' is ever outside the valid enum range [0, CLI_HASH_AVAIL_TYPES) — e.g. from an uninitialized or corrupted caller value — every one of these becomes an out-of-bounds read, and a garbage 'keylen' then drives the stride arithmetic and hm_cmp length at line 22 (turning it into an OOB read of arbitrary width). The invariant is that the caller passes a validated enum value; this function does no range check on 'type'. Improper array-index validation, CWE-129 / CWE-125.

The found element's data union is blindly reinterpreted as a cli_sz_hash pointer with no NULL check. If any insertion path can register a size key whose data.as_ptr is NULL (or a differently-typed payload), the very next uses — szh->items at line 19 and szh->hash_array at line 22 — dereference that pointer, producing a NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476) or type-confusion read. The invariant: every element stored in root->hm.sizehashes[type] carries a valid, non-NULL cli_sz_hash pointer in data.as_ptr.

Standard binary-search midpoint. On its own it is safe because l and r stay small when szh->items is a sane count. However, it is the amplifier for the line-19 underflow: if r == SIZE_MAX then (l+r) itself can overflow size_t (SIZE_MAX + 0), and the resulting 'c' selects an out-of-bounds element. The invariant that keeps this expression safe is exactly the 'items >= 1' precondition from the line-19 discussion — there is no independent overflow risk once r is a valid in-array bound.

summary: A ClamAV size-keyed hash matcher that binary-searches a per-size sorted hash array. The shown code is defensively written (it explicitly rejects the 0 and 0xffffffff sentinel sizes and guards the c==0 unsigned decrement), so it appears to be a hardened/fixed sample. The one genuine latent hazard is 'r = szh->items - 1' on line 19: it never checks szh->items > 0, so an empty cli_sz_hash record underflows r to SIZE_MAX and drives an out-of-bounds read; the remaining risks are caller/loader contracts (digest length vs keylen, enum 'type' range, non-NULL data.as_ptr).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: cli_hm_scan(const unsigned char *digest, uint32_t size, const char **virname, const struct cli_matcher *root, enum CLI_HASH_TYPE type) — line 2
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry signature; multiple parameters carry security contracts (digest length, size sentinel, type range).
Length is implicit (hashlen[type]); no length parameter, so hm_cmp reads keylen bytes on trust — OOB-read contract.
Attacker-influenced key; collides with reserved 0/0xffffffff sentinels — validation gate at line 8.
Trusted caller output pointer; written only after a NULL check (line 31), no attacker control.
Trusted signature DB pointer; NULL-checked on line 8.
Used as an unchecked array subscript into hashlen[] and sizehashes[]; OOB if out of enum range.
NULL-checked at line 12 before use.
Derived scalar (stride/length); its risk is inherited from hashlen[type] indexing, covered by the 'type' anchor.
Set from item->data.as_ptr with no NULL/type check; later dereferenced (items, hash_array).
r initialized by an unsigned subtraction (line 19) that can underflow to SIZE_MAX.
Validation gate; the 0xffffffff sentinel exclusion is the numeric-error contract and is correctly enforced.
Checked non-zero before the lookup; the 'type' subscript risk is tracked under the 'type' anchor.
Hashtable lookup; result NULL-checked (line 12), and dangerous keys are excluded by line 8.
Reinterpreted as cli_sz_hash* with no NULL check before dereference — CWE-476 contract.
Global-array index by unchecked enum 'type'; OOB read if type out of range.
Unsigned underflow to SIZE_MAX when items==0; no items>0 check — primary latent OOB-read primitive.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: while(l <= r) binary search loop — lines 20-35
reviewed_anchors.reason: Search bounds/stride depend on szh->items and keylen; correctness of every access rests on those invariants.
Safe midpoint when r is a valid bound; only overflows as a consequence of the line-19 underflow, which is filed separately.
Reads keylen bytes from digest and from hash_array; bounds depend on digest length, keylen, and c staying in range.
Correctly prevents unsigned wrap of 'c - 1' when c==0; a defensive fix, no defect.
Guarded by the preceding if(!c) break, so no unsigned underflow here.
Parallel array indexed by the same in-range 'c' as hash_array; safe under the same items invariant already filed.

findings.title: Unsigned underflow of binary-search right bound when szh->items == 0 (out-of-bounds read)
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow feeding out-of-bounds array read
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0321_cwe189.c (and the loader that populates szh->items — implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every cli_sz_hash record reachable from root->hm.sizehashes[type] must have items >= 1, OR cli_hm_scan must reject items==0 (e.g. 'if(!szh->items) return CL_CLEAN;') before computing r = items - 1.
findings.failure_mode: If a size-hash record with items==0 is found, 'szh->items - 1' wraps (size_t) to SIZE_MAX. 'while(l <= r)' is then true, c becomes ~SIZE_MAX/2, and 'hm_cmp(digest, &szh->hash_array[keylen*c], keylen)' at line 22 reads memory far outside hash_array — a wild out-of-bounds read that can crash the scanner or leak memory contents into the comparison result. The capacity check on line 8 does not cover szh->items.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether the DB-load path can ever insert a cli_sz_hash with items==0 (e.g. an allocated-but-unfilled bucket), and whether any code path decrements items. If items==0 is reachable, add an explicit guard before line 19.

findings.title: Hash type enum used as an unchecked array subscript into hashlen[] and sizehashes[]
findings.bug_class: Improper validation of array index (out-of-bounds read)
findings.invariant: 'type' must be a valid CLI_HASH_TYPE in [0, number_of_hash_types) on every call; cli_hm_scan performs no range check and trusts the caller.
findings.failure_mode: An out-of-range or uninitialized 'type' reads hashlen[] (line 16) and root->hm.sizehashes[] (lines 8, 11) out of bounds. A garbage keylen then sets the hm_cmp comparison length and the hash_array stride on line 22, potentially turning the comparison into an arbitrary-width OOB read. Impact depends on whether any caller can be induced to pass an invalid type.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check that all call sites pass a validated enum value and that hashlen[] and sizehashes[] have an entry for every CLI_HASH_TYPE the callers can produce; consider an explicit range assert at function entry.

findings.title: digest length is implicit — hm_cmp reads keylen bytes from a caller-sized buffer
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read of the digest buffer (missing length coupling)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0321_cwe189.c (digest buffer allocated by caller — implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: The buffer behind 'digest' must be at least hashlen[type] bytes, matching the 'type' passed in the same call; there is no length parameter enforcing this inside cli_hm_scan.
findings.failure_mode: If a caller computes 'digest' for one hash length but passes a 'type' with a larger hashlen (or passes a short/truncated digest), hm_cmp reads keylen bytes past the end of the digest allocation, an out-of-bounds read. The hash_array side is in bounds only if the record holds items*keylen bytes and 'c' is in [0, items-1].
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that every caller allocates digest as exactly hashlen[type] bytes for the same 'type' argument, and that hash_array is allocated as items*keylen for the record's key length.

findings.title: item->data.as_ptr dereferenced as cli_sz_hash* without NULL/type check
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0321_cwe189.c (hashtable insertion path — implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every element stored under root->hm.sizehashes[type] must carry a valid, non-NULL cli_sz_hash pointer in data.as_ptr.
findings.failure_mode: If any insertion can leave data.as_ptr NULL (or store a differently-typed payload in the union), the immediate uses szh->items (line 19) and szh->hash_array (line 22) dereference a NULL/wrong pointer, crashing the scanner or reading unintended memory.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the insertion path always sets data.as_ptr to a valid cli_sz_hash; if not guaranteed, add a '!szh' check after line 15.

findings.title: Sentinel-size validation gate (0 and 0xffffffff excluded before lookup) — present and correct
findings.invariant: Reserved key values (0 and 0xffffffff, the cli_htu32 sentinels) must never be passed to cli_htu32_find as a real lookup key; an object whose true size equals a sentinel must be treated as no-match.
findings.failure_mode: This is the CWE-189/numeric contract the sample turns on: were the 'size == 0xffffffff' clause removed, an attacker could craft an object of exactly 0xffffffff bytes so the value is used as a live key, colliding with the hashtable's reserved sentinel and potentially returning a spurious 'item' whose data.as_ptr is not a valid cli_sz_hash. As written the gate blocks this, so it is reported as a correctly-enforced check, not an open defect.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm 0 and 0xffffffff are indeed the reserved empty/deleted markers of cli_htu32 and that no other reserved sentinel values exist that should also be excluded here.

audit_checklist: Prove szh->items >= 1 for every record reachable via root->hm.sizehashes[type], or add 'if(!szh->items) return CL_CLEAN;' before 'r = szh->items - 1;' (line 19).
Verify hash_array for each record is allocated as items * hashlen[type] bytes so &hash_array[keylen*c] with c in [0, items-1] is always in bounds.
Confirm every caller passes a validated CLI_HASH_TYPE and a 'digest' buffer of exactly hashlen[type] bytes (couples the hm_cmp keylen read to the allocation).
Confirm hashlen[] and root->hm.sizehashes[] have an entry for every CLI_HASH_TYPE any caller can produce (no OOB on the 'type' subscript).
Confirm cli_htu32's reserved sentinels are exactly {0, 0xffffffff} and that the line-8 gate excludes all of them before cli_htu32_find.
Confirm the insertion path always stores a non-NULL, correctly-typed cli_sz_hash pointer in item->data.as_ptr.
Verify hm_cmp compares exactly keylen bytes and reads neither operand past its buffer.
Confirm the hash_array is sorted with the same ordering hm_cmp implements, so the binary search cannot skip a present entry (correctness, not memory-safety).
